title: Resource-efficient energy-based operator selection in fermionic ADAPT-VQE via exact Hamiltonian transformation
authors: Emanuele Rossi, Erik Rosendahl Kjellgren, Artur F. Izmaylov, Stephan P. A. Sauer, Karl Michael Ziems, Sonia Coriani
abstract: The energy-based approach to operator selection in ADAPT-VQE relies on reconstructing the one-parameter energy landscape for each operator in the pool. In fermionic implementations, the cost of reconstructing this energy landscape often becomes a bottleneck. We address this issue through an exact Hamiltonian transformation that reformulates the one-parameter energy landscape according to a generator-dependent fragmentation of the transformed Hamiltonian. While our method is mathematically identical to standard fermionic Rotoselect, it effectively reduces its cost by about a factor of two, bringing it close to that of gradient-based ADAPT-VQE. We use this formulation to benchmark the gradient-based and energy-based selection approaches in combination with two ansatz-optimization strategies -- `last', where only the appended operator is optimized, or `full', where the full ansatz is re-optimized -- and with both fixed-orbital and orbital-optimized formulations. The benchmark comprises $\text{LiH}$, $\text{BeH}_2$, and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ at both equilibrium and stretched geometries. In the weakly correlated regime, the `last' optimization strategy combined with energy-based selection enables the efficient construction of an accurate ansatz, while avoiding any VQE optimization. As correlation increases, full ansatz re-optimization and orbital optimization become the main factors governing convergence and overall resource cost. These results show that exact Hamiltonian transformations provide an effective route to reducing the measurement overhead of fermionic energy-based ADAPT-VQE. Moreover, the benchmark clarifies the relative role of operator scoring approach, re-optimization strategy, and orbital treatment in the performance of ADAPT-VQE.
summary: This paper improves the resource efficiency of energy-based operator selection in fermionic ADAPT-VQE by introducing an exact Hamiltonian transformation. This technique significantly reduces the measurement overhead required to score potential operators, making the selection process competitive with gradient-based methods. The results demonstrate that this efficient approach helps achieve accurate quantum chemical results for molecules even in highly correlated regimes.
main_problem: The primary challenge addressed is the computational bottleneck associated with reconstructing the one-parameter energy landscape during energy-based operator selection in fermionic ADAPT-VQE.
main_result: The authors introduce an exact Hamiltonian transformation that reformulates the energy landscape, reducing the measurement overhead of energy-based selection by about a factor of two, bringing it close to gradient-based methods.
method: The method involves using an exact Hamiltonian transformation to efficiently calculate the energy cost function $E^{(n+1)}_g(	heta_g)$ for operator selection, which is then used within the ADAPT-VQE framework.
model_or_system: The study is applied to fermionic quantum chemistry calculations for molecules like LiH, BeH2, and H2O, using the electronic Hamiltonian in second quantization.
key_observables: Energy convergence relative to the FCI reference, selection cost (measured in circuit evaluations), and overall resource cost.
important_parameters: The comparison spans weakly correlated (equilibrium) and highly correlated (stretched) molecular geometries.
main_assumptions: The efficiency gain relies on the mathematical identity derived from the exact Hamiltonian transformation, which simplifies the calculation of the energy cost function.
figures_summary: Figures compare the energy convergence and the relative cost (selection vs. optimization) of various ADAPT-VQE strategies (Gradient vs. Energy-based) across different molecular geometries.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the ADAPT-VQE framework and the cost of energy-based selection. It then details the exact Hamiltonian transformation to derive a resource-efficient energy cost function. Finally, it benchmarks this improved method against gradient-based approaches across various molecular systems and optimization strategies.
why_interesting: This work directly tackles the resource scaling of quantum chemistry simulations, which is crucial for applying VQE to complex, strongly correlated molecular systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
